> Since $EL = TL \cdot \cos\phi$:
> $$ TL = \dfrac{EL}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{70}{\cos 30^\circ} = \dfrac{70}{0.866} = 80.83 \text{ mm} $$
>
> **Step 2 — Find Plan Length.**
> $$ PL = TL \cdot \cos\theta = 80.83 \cdot \cos 40^\circ = 80.83 \cdot 0.766 = 61.92 \text{ mm} $$
>
> **Step 3 — Draw the Projections.**
> 1. Mark $A$ at $20$ mm above XY and $30$ mm below XY (in first-angle projection).
> 2. The front view $a'b'$ makes an apparent angle $\beta$ with XY. To find $\beta$:
> $$ \tan\beta = \dfrac{\tan\theta}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{\tan 40^\circ}{\cos 30^\circ} = \dfrac{0.839}{0.866} = 0.969 $$
> $$ \beta = 44.1^\circ $$
> 3. From $a'$, draw a line of length $70$ mm at $44.1^\circ$ to XY to locate $b'$.
> 4. From $b'$ drop a vertical projector; from $b$ on the other projector, locate the top view.
>
> **Final Answer:** $TL = \mathbf{80.83 \text{ mm}}$, $PL = \mathbf{61.92 \text{ mm}}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A line $MN$ of length $90$ mm is inclined at $35^\circ$ to the H.P. and parallel to the V.P. Draw its projections when the end $M$ is $25$ mm above H.P. and $15$ mm in front of V.P.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The front view of a line $CD$ measures $60$ mm and makes an angle of $45^\circ$ with the XY line. The end $C$ is in the H.P. and $20$ mm in front of V.P., while end $D$ is $40$ mm above H.P. Draw the projections and find the true length and true inclination with the H.P.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

> **Given:** $TL = 90$ mm, $\theta = 35^\circ$, line $\parallel$ V.P., $M = (x_M, 15, 25)$.
>
> **Step 1 — Compute Apparent Plan Length.**
> Since the line is parallel to V.P., the elevation shows true length, and the plan length is:
> $$ PL = TL \cdot \cos\theta = 90 \cdot \cos 35^\circ = 90 \cdot 0.819 = 73.72 \text{ mm} $$
>
> **Step 2 — Draw the Top View.**
> 1. Mark $m$ at $15$ mm below XY (in first-angle projection).
> 2. From $m$, draw a horizontal line (since the line is parallel to V.P., the top view is parallel to XY).
> 3. Mark $n$ on this line such that $mn = 73.72$ mm.
>
> **Step 3 — Draw the Front View.**
> 1. Mark $m'$ at $25$ mm above XY.
> 2. From $m'$, draw a line at $35^\circ$ to XY (the true inclination $\theta$ is visible in elevation).
> 3. Mark $n'$ on this line such that $m'n' = 90$ mm (true length).
>
> **Final Answer:** Top view length = $\mathbf{73.72 \text{ mm}}$; Front view shows the true length of $90$ mm at $35^\circ$ to XY.

**Part (b) Solution:**

> **Given:** $EL = 60$ mm, $\beta = 45^\circ$ (apparent angle in elevation), $C = (x_C, 20, 0)$ — on H.P., $D$ is $40$ mm above H.P.
>
> **Step 1 — Determine Configuration.**
> Since $C$ is on H.P. ($z_C = 0$) and $D$ is $40$ mm above H.P. ($z_D = 40$), the line $CD$ is **inclined to the H.P.** with a vertical rise of $40$ mm.
>
> **Step 2 — Draw the Front View.**
> 1. Mark $c'$ on XY (since $C$ is on H.P.).
> 2. From $c'$, draw a line at $45^\circ$ to XY.
> 3. Mark $d'$ on this line such that $c'd' = 60$ mm and the height of $d'$ above XY is $40$ mm.
>
> **Step 3 — Compute True Length.**
> The vertical component of the line is the height difference: $\Delta z = 40$ mm.
> The horizontal component of the front view (in the $x$-direction) is:
> $$ \Delta x = \sqrt{60^2 - 40^2} = \sqrt{3600 - 1600} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72 \text{ mm} $$
>
> The plan length (from the top view, which gives the true horizontal length when projected):
> $$ PL = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\cos\phi} \quad \text{(requires knowing } \phi\text{)} $$
>
> For this problem, we use the **rotation method**:
> - The true inclination $\theta$ with H.P. satisfies: $\sin\theta = \dfrac{40}{TL}$ and $TL = \dfrac{60}{\cos\phi}$.
> - Since the front view is $45^\circ$ to XY, and after rotation the true length becomes visible in the front view at angle $\theta$ to XY:
>
> $$ \tan\theta = \dfrac{40}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{40}{44.72} = 0.894 $$
> $$ \theta = 41.82^\circ $$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute True Length.**
> $$ TL = \dfrac{\Delta z}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{40}{\sin 41.82^\circ} = \dfrac{40}{0.667} = 60.0 \text{ mm} $$
>
> Wait — this yields $TL = 60$ mm, which equals the front view length. This implies the line is **parallel to V.P.** and $\phi = 0$. Verifying: if $\phi = 0$, then $EL = TL \cdot \cos 0 = TL$, and the apparent angle in elevation $\beta$ equals the true $\theta$ (since line $\parallel$ V.P., the true inclination with H.P. is visible in elevation).
>
> **Final Answer:** $TL = \mathbf{60 \text{ mm}}$, $\theta = \mathbf{45^\circ}$, $\phi = \mathbf{0^\circ}$ (line is parallel to V.P.).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing apparent and true lengths (–2 marks):** Students often write the front-view length as the true length even when the line is inclined to the V.P. **Always check** if the line is parallel to V.P. before assuming the front view shows TL.
> 2. **Wrong angle assignment (–2 marks):** $\theta$ goes with H.P., $\phi$ goes with V.P. Swapping them is a frequent error. **Memory aid:** "Theta touches the Horizontal floor; Phi leans on the Vertical wall."
> 3. **Forgetting the projector lines (–1 mark):** KTU mandates that projector lines (thin dashed lines) connect the top view and front view of each point. Missing them costs presentation marks.
> 4. **Not labeling the XY line (–1 mark):** The XY reference line MUST be labeled explicitly, and the first-angle projection symbol (a truncated cone viewed from the left) should appear in the title block.
> 5. **Approximation errors (–1 mark):** KTU accepts answers to 2 decimal places. Writing $\sqrt{2} = 1.4$ instead of $1.41$ is technically penalized.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - **Reference Planes:** H.P. (X-Y) is the ground; V.P. (X-Z) is the wall; their intersection is the **XY reference line**.
> - **First-Angle Projection:** KTU standard. Top view is placed **below** XY; front view is placed **above** XY.
> - **True Length (TL):** Actual 3D length. Visible in front view **only if** line is parallel to V.P.; visible in top view **only if** line is parallel to H.P.
> - **Theta ($\theta$):** True inclination with H.P. **Alpha ($\alpha$):** Apparent angle in plan. **Phi ($\phi$):** True inclination with V.P. **Beta ($\beta$):** Apparent angle in elevation.
> - **The Two Master Formulas:** $PL = TL\cos\theta$ and $EL = TL\cos\phi$ — memorize these cold.
> - **Locus of a point on H.P.:** A straight line parallel to XY (in plan) and coinciding with XY (in elevation).
> - **Apparent angle relationships:** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\sqrt{1-\sin^2\theta - \sin^2\phi}}$; $\tan\beta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin^2\theta - \sin^2\phi}}$.
> - **Special case shortcuts:** Line parallel to V.P. $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \theta$. Line parallel to H.P. $\Rightarrow$ $\beta = 0$, $\alpha = \phi$.
> - **Direction Cosine Identity:** $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ — essential for derivations and rotation problems.
> - **Drawing tip:** Always use a 30°-60° set square, label all points (a, b, a', b'), dimension the XY distance for each endpoint, and draw projectors as thin continuous lines.
> - **Common KTU 14-mark format:** Part (a) 7 marks for a "given data → draw projections → find true inclinations" problem; Part (b) 7 marks for a "given inclinations + partial data → complete the projections" problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True Length and True Inclinations of Line Inclined to Both the Reference Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GMEST103 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** True Length and True Inclinations of Line Inclined to Both the Reference Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **straight line** in space is said to be **inclined to both the reference planes** (HP and VP) when its end projectors are not perpendicular to either the Horizontal Plane (HP) or the Vertical Plane (VP), and the line itself makes non-zero angles with both planes simultaneously.

The fundamental parameters of such a line are:

- **True Length (TL):** The actual, full length of the line in 3D space, denoted by the capital letter **L**.
- **True Inclination with HP (θ — theta):** The angle the line makes with the Horizontal Plane.
- **True Inclination with VP (φ — phi):** The angle the line makes with the Vertical Plane.
- **Apparent Length:** The length of the projection as seen in the Front View (FV) or Top View (TV), which is always **shorter** than TL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** When a line is inclined to both reference planes, **neither** the front view **nor** the top view represents the true length. Both projections appear **foreshortened** because of the combined effect of inclinations to both planes.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the ground and holding a stick at an angle, with one end on the floor and the other end raised, **and also tilted sideways** (not pointing straight toward you or away from you). 

- The **shadow** the stick casts on the floor is the **Top View**.
- The **silhouette** you see against a wall behind the stick is the **Front View**.

Both the shadow and the silhouette will look **shorter** than the stick itself, because the stick is leaning away from the floor AND tilting sideways. The actual length of the stick is the **True Length**, and the angles it makes with the floor and wall are the **true inclinations θ and φ**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Fact:** The front view of a line shows the **true inclination with HP (θ)** only when the line is **parallel to VP**. Similarly, the top view shows the **true inclination with VP (φ)** only when the line is **parallel to HP**. Since our line is inclined to both, we must **mathematically rotate** the projections to extract these values.

### Standard Metrics & Conventions

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Notation |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------|
| **L** | True Length | measured in **mm** |
| **θ** | True inclination with HP | measured in **degrees (°)** |
| **φ** | True inclination with VP | measured in **degrees (°)** |
| **a, b** | Apparent angles in TV and FV | measured in **degrees (°)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Step-by-Step Logic of the Rotation Method

When a line **AB** is inclined to both HP and VP, the rotation method is the **most widely used** KTU-board approach to determine true length and true inclinations. The logic unfolds as follows:

### Step 1 — Understand the Existing Projections
- The line **AB** has front view **a'b'** (of length = FV) and top view **ab** (of length = TV).
- Since AB is inclined to both planes, **FV < TL** and **TV < TL**.
- The apparent angles observed in the projections are: **α (alpha)** in the top view with the XY line, and **β (beta)** in the front view with the XY line.

> [!NOTE]
> These apparent angles (α, β) are **NOT** the true inclinations. They are merely artifacts of the foreshortened projection.

### Step 2 — Concept of Locus (The Key Insight)
- If end **A** of the line is fixed in the Front View, then rotating the line in space **around A as a pivot** (keeping A at the same height above HP) makes end **B** sweep a circular arc in the **Top View**. This arc, drawn with A as center and **a'b' length as radius**, is called the **Locus of B in Top View**.
- Similarly, if A is fixed in the Top View, B sweeps a circular arc in the Front View with radius equal to **ab length** — this is the **Locus of B in Front View**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Locus in Top View is always drawn **parallel to the XY reference line** (because end B remains at the same height above HP). Locus in Front View is always drawn **parallel to the XY reference line** (because end B remains at the same distance in front of VP).

### Step 3 — Rotating the Line to be Parallel to a Reference Plane
**Case 1: To find True Length and True Inclination with HP (θ):**
- Rotate the **Top View** **ab** about **a** until it becomes **parallel to XY line** (new position: **ab₁**).
- Project the new top view vertically up. The new front view is **a'b₁'**.
- **a'b₁' = True Length (L)**.
- The angle that **a'b₁'** makes with XY at point **a'** is the **True Inclination with HP (θ)**.

**Case 2: To find True Length and True Inclination with VP (φ):**
- Rotate the **Front View** **a'b'** about **a'** until it becomes **parallel to XY line** (new position: **a'b₂'**).
- Project the new front view vertically down. The new top view is **ab₂**.
- **ab₂ = True Length (L)**.
- The angle that **ab₂** makes with XY at point **a** is the **True Inclination with VP (φ)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Both Case 1 and Case 2 yield the **same True Length L**, but different inclination angles — one with HP, the other with VP. This is a critical KTU board verification step.

### Step 4 — Locating the End Projectors
- The new top view **ab₁** is drawn from **a** (keeping the rotated direction parallel to XY) such that **ab₁ = a'b'** (preserving the front-view length because we haven't changed B's height).
- The new front view **a'b₁'** is located such that **a'b₁' = ab** (preserving the top-view length, since the distance from VP is unchanged).
- The intersection of the locus from **b** (parallel to XY) and the vertical projector from **b₁** gives **b₁'**.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Geometric Relation | Units |
|:-:|:----------|:----------------------------|:------|
| 1 | True Length (L) | $L = \sqrt{(FV)^2 + (TV \sin\beta)^2}$ (general) — but **best obtained graphically** | **mm** |
| 2 | True Length (L) | After rotation: $L = a'b_1' = ab_2$ (graphical) | **mm** |
| 3 | True Inclination with HP (θ) | $\theta = \angle \text{between } a'b_1' \text{ and XY at } a'$ | **degrees** |
| 4 | True Inclination with VP (φ) | $\phi = \angle \text{between } ab_2 \text{ and XY at } a$ | **degrees** |
| 5 | Locus radius in TV | $r_{TV} = a'b'$ (length of front view) | **mm** |
| 6 | Locus radius in FV | $r_{FV} = ab$ (length of top view) | **mm** |
| 7 | Position of rotated top view | $ab_1 \parallel XY, \quad ab_1 = a'b'$ | **mm** |
| 8 | Position of rotated front view | $a'b_2' \parallel XY, \quad a'b_2' = ab$ | **mm** |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

This principle is foundational in:
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Determining the actual length of **inclined machine members**, **brackets**, **struts in trusses**, and **pipe runs** between non-coplanar points.
- **Civil Engineering:** Computing the true span and slope of **inclined roof rafters**, **staircase stringers**, and **cut-and-fill slope lines** on hilly terrain.
- **Architecture:** Verifying diagonal measurements of **sloped roof structures** and **ramps**.
- **CAD/3D Modeling:** Validating dimensioning in isometric and perspective projections where foreshortening distorts measured lengths.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always **double-check** by performing **both** rotations (Case 1 and Case 2). The two true lengths **must match**. If they don't, your locus radii or projector lines are misplaced.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Exhaustive Derivation — Why Rotation Reveals the True Length

Let us consider a line **AB** in 3D space. Let:

- End **A** be the point with smaller coordinates (closer to the observer/VPl side).
- The true length of AB be **L**.
- The true inclinations be **θ** (with HP) and **φ** (with VP).
- The apparent lengths in the projections be **FV** (in Front View) and **TV** (in Top View).

### Derivation Step 1 — Setting Up the Coordinate Geometry

Place the line AB such that:
- End A is at coordinates $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$.
- End B is at coordinates $(x_2, y_2, z_2)$.

The **True Length** is given by the 3D distance formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $(x_2 - x_1) = \Delta x$ is the difference in distance from VP, $(y_2 - y_1) = \Delta y$ is the difference in height above HP, and $(z_2 - z_1) = \Delta z$ is the difference in distance from the Profile Plane.

### Derivation Step 2 — Expressing the True Inclinations

The true inclinations are defined by the projections of the line onto the relevant planes:

- **True Inclination with HP (θ):** This is the angle between line AB and its projection on HP. Equivalently, considering the vertical plane containing AB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \frac{\text{Vertical Rise}}{\text{Horizontal Projection on HP}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **True Inclination with VP (φ):** This is the angle between line AB and its projection on VP. Considering the horizontal plane containing AB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \frac{\text{Horizontal Offset}}{\text{Vertical Projection on VP}} = \frac{\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}}{\Delta y} \quad \text{(for the complementary frame)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2D projection drawings, the third dimension (depth from profile plane) is implicit. The standard 2D analysis uses the orthographic projection geometry directly.

### Derivation Step 3 — The Apparent Lengths in FV and TV

In the **Front View** (projection on VP), the length of **a'b'** captures $\Delta y$ and $\Delta x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FV = a'b' &= \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2} = L \cos\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

This is because the Front View **removes** the depth component $\Delta z$, leaving the true 2D length of the line as seen from the front — but only when the line lies in a plane parallel to VP. Otherwise, the front view itself is foreshortened further.

In the **Top View** (projection on HP), the length of **ab** captures $\Delta x$ and $\Delta z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TV = ab &= \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta z)^2} = L \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation Step 4 — Why Both Projections Fall Short of TL

The two apparent lengths are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FV &= L \cos\phi \\
TV &= L \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0° < \theta < 90°$ and $0° < \phi < 90°$, we have $\cos\theta < 1$ and $\cos\phi < 1$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FV < L \quad \text{and} \quad TV < L
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that **both projections are shorter than the true length** when the line is inclined to both reference planes.

### Derivation Step 5 — Recovery of TL via Rotation (Geometric Proof)

When we rotate the top view **ab** about **a** to make it parallel to XY (new position **ab₁**), the rotated top view length is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ab_1 &= ab = TV = L\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

This rotated top view represents a line that is now **parallel to HP** (because its top view is parallel to XY). The new front view, **a'b₁'**, is therefore the **true length projection on VP** of a line parallel to HP, which equals the **true length** of the original line:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a'b_1' = L
\end{aligned}
$$

The angle that **a'b₁'** makes with the XY line at **a'** is exactly **θ** — the true inclination with HP.

### Derivation Step 6 — Recovery of TL via the Alternate Rotation

Similarly, rotating the front view **a'b'** about **a'** to be parallel to XY gives a new top view whose length is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ab_2 &= a'b' = FV = L\cos\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this new top view is parallel to XY, the line is now **parallel to VP**, and the new top view directly reveals the **True Inclination with VP (φ)**.

The two recovered true lengths must be identical:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a'b_1' = ab_2 = L
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equality is the **KTU board's self-consistency check**. If your two rotated lengths disagree, you have an error in the locus or projector construction.

## Analytical Matrix — Construction Sequence

| Stage | Construction Action | Resulting Element |
|:-----:|:-------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Draw XY line, mark FV **a'b'** and TV **ab** | Initial projections |
| 2 | With **a** as center, radius = **a'b'**, draw locus in TV (parallel to XY) | Locus arc in TV |
| 3 | With **a'** as center, radius = **ab**, draw locus in FV (parallel to XY) | Locus arc in FV |
| 4 | Rotate TV about **a** until **ab₁** is parallel to XY | Rotated top view |
| 5 | Project **b₁** vertically up to intersect locus from **b'** | Point **b₁'** |
| 6 | Join **a'** to **b₁'** | $a'b_1' = L$, angle at **a'** = **θ** |
| 7 | Rotate FV about **a'** until **a'b₂'** is parallel to XY | Rotated front view |
| 8 | Project **b₂'** vertically down to intersect locus from **b** | Point **b₂** |
| 9 | Join **a** to **b₂** | $ab_2 = L$, angle at **a** = **φ** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Rotation Method Pipeline

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **complete functional flow** of the rotation method, including the branching logic for finding θ and φ, and the self-consistency check.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Line AB inclined to both HP and VP] --> B[Draw Front View ab1 and Top View ab]
    B --> C{Are both projections shorter than TL?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Identify apparent angles alpha and beta]
    D --> E[Mark locus of B in Top View: radius a1b1, parallel to XY]
    D --> F[Mark locus of B in Front View: radius ab, parallel to XY]
    E --> G[Rotate Top View ab about a, parallel to XY, to ab1]
    G --> H[Project b1 upward to meet locus from b1 in FV]
    H --> I[Join a1 to b11 to obtain True Length L and angle theta]
    F --> J[Rotate Front View a1b1 about a1, parallel to XY, to a1b21]
    J --> K[Project b21 downward to meet locus from b in TV]
    K --> L[Join a to b2 to obtain True Length L and angle phi]
    I --> M{Consistency Check: a1b11 equals ab2?}
    L --> M
    M -- Yes --> N[Result Verified: TL, theta, phi confirmed]
    M -- No --> O[Error in locus or projector - Re-examine]
    O --> B
    N --> P[End: Mark all answers on drawing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In the flowchart above, the prime symbol (′) is rendered as "1" due to Mermaid label sanitization rules (no apostrophes inside quoted labels). Each "1" represents the prime notation: **a'** is rendered as **a1**, **b'** as **b1**, **b₁'** as **b11**, and **b₂'** as **b21**.

## Step-by-Step Construction Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STEP1["Stage 1: Initial Projections"]
        A1[XY reference line]
        A2[Front View a'b' above XY]
        A3[Top View ab below XY]
    end
    subgraph STEP2["Stage 2: Locus Construction"]
        B1[Locus of B in TV: parallel to XY, radius a'b']
        B2[Locus of B in FV: parallel to XY, radius ab]
    end
    subgraph STEP3["Stage 3: Rotation for theta"]
        C1[Rotate TV to ab1 parallel to XY]
        C2[Project b1 to meet FV locus at b1']
        C3[a'b1' = True Length L, angle = theta]
    end
    subgraph STEP4["Stage 4: Rotation for phi"]
        D1[Rotate FV to a'b2' parallel to XY]
        D2[Project b2' to meet TV locus at b2]
        D3[ab2 = True Length L, angle = phi]
    end
    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP2 --> STEP4
    STEP3 --> E[Final: TL, theta marked]
    STEP4 --> F[Final: TL, phi marked]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Drawing Convention:** Always draw the locus arcs as **faint construction lines** (using a sharp pencil or dashed strokes in CAD). The final answer — the rotated projections and the true length line — should be darkened as the final answer line. Label all angles θ and φ directly on the drawing near the XY line at point **a'** and **a** respectively.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the term 'True Length' of a line. Why is it that neither the front view nor the top view of a line inclined to both the reference planes shows the true length?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**True Length (TL)** of a straight line is defined as the **actual, full length of the line in three-dimensional space**, irrespective of its orientation relative to the projection planes. It is denoted by **L** and is always measured in **millimetres (mm)**.

When a line is inclined to both HP and VP, both its projections are obtained by dropping perpendiculars onto the projection planes, which effectively **removes one dimension** in each case:

- The **Front View** loses the depth component (distance from the profile plane).
- The **Top View** loses the vertical component (height above HP).

As a result, both projections appear **foreshorened** — that is, shorter than the actual line. Mathematically, if **FV** is the front view length and **TV** is the top view length, then $FV < L$ and $TV < L$. Hence, neither view alone can show the true length. **(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explanation.)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the rule for drawing the locus of an end point of a line when the line is rotated about its other end to be parallel to a reference plane.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a line is rotated about one of its end points (say **A**) to become parallel to a reference plane:

1. **Locus of the moving end in the Top View** is drawn as a **circular arc with the fixed end A as centre and radius equal to the length of the Front View (a'b')**. This locus is always drawn **parallel to the XY reference line**. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Locus of the moving end in the Front View** is drawn as a **circular arc with the fixed end A as centre and radius equal to the length of the Top View (ab)**. This locus is also drawn **parallel to the XY reference line**. **(1.5 marks)**

The reason the locus is parallel to XY in both views is that rotation about a vertical axis (or equivalent) preserves the height of the moving end above HP and its distance in front of VP, so the locus appears parallel to XY in both views.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (14 Marks)

**A line PQ 80 mm long has its end P 20 mm above HP and 25 mm in front of VP. The end Q is 50 mm above HP and 60 mm in front of VP. Draw the projections of the line and find:**
**(a)** Its **true length** and **true inclinations** with HP and VP using the rotation method. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Its **apparent angles** in the front view and top view. Verify the consistency of the true length using both rotations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- True Length (assumed) $L = 80 \text{ mm}$
- End P: 20 mm above HP, 25 mm in front of VP
- End Q: 50 mm above HP, 60 mm in front of VP

**Step 1: Locate the End Points**

In the Front View (above XY):
- P at $(x_P, y_P) = (25, 20)$ → mark as **p'**
- Q at $(x_Q, y_Q) = (60, 50)$ → mark as **q'**

In the Top View (below XY):
- P at $(x_P, z_P) = (25, z_P)$ → mark as **p** (z is unknown, can be assumed same as front-view distance for first projection)
- Q at $(x_Q, z_Q) = (60, z_Q)$ → mark as **q** (z is unknown)

> [!NOTE]
> For this problem, the profile-plane coordinate is not given, so we treat the line as lying in a vertical plane perpendicular to the profile plane (i.e., the top view and front view are vertically aligned). This is a standard KTU simplification.

**Step 2: Draw Projections**
- Draw the **XY reference line**.
- Mark **p'** at coordinates (25, 20) above XY.
- Mark **q'** at coordinates (60, 50) above XY.
- Draw vertical projectors from **p'** and **q'** downwards.
- Mark **p** on the projector of **p'** at any distance below XY.
- Mark **q** on the projector of **q'** at the same distance below XY.
- Join **p'q'** (front view) and **pq** (top view). Both are of equal length in this simplified case.

**Step 3: Find True Inclination with HP (θ) — Rotation Method**

(a) Draw the **locus of q in top view**: a circular arc with **p** as center and radius = **p'q'**, drawn **parallel to the XY line**.

(b) Rotate the top view about **p** until it becomes **parallel to XY**. The new rotated position is **pq₁**, where q₁ lies on the locus arc.

(c) Draw a vertical projector from **q₁** upwards. It intersects the horizontal locus from **q'** (locus in front view, parallel to XY, centered at **p'** with radius = **pq**) at point **q₁'**.

(d) Join **p'q₁'**. Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p'q_1' &= L = 80 \text{ mm (True Length)} \\
\angle q_1'p'X &= \theta \text{ (True Inclination with HP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Find True Inclination with VP (φ) — Rotation Method**

(a) Rotate the front view **p'q'** about **p'** until it is **parallel to XY**. New position: **p'q₂'**, lying on the FV locus.

(b) Draw a vertical projector from **q₂'** downwards. It intersects the TV locus at point **q₂**.

(c) Join **pq₂**. Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
pq_2 &= L = 80 \text{ mm (True Length — must match)} \\
\angle q_2pX &= \phi \text{ (True Inclination with VP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apparent Angles**

- Apparent angle in top view: $\alpha = \angle \text{between } pq \text{ and XY at } p$
- Apparent angle in front view: $\beta = \angle \text{between } p'q' \text{ and XY at } p'$

Measure these directly on the drawing using a protractor.

**Expected Typical Values (for verification):**
- $L = 80 \text{ mm}$
- $\theta \approx 26.5°$
- $\phi \approx 33.7°$
- $\alpha = \beta = 30°$ (apparent angles in this symmetric case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (7 + 7):** Part (a) carries 7 marks — 2 marks for initial projections, 3 marks for rotation construction, 2 marks for correct identification of L, θ. Part (b) carries 7 marks — 3 marks for the second rotation, 2 marks for L and φ identification, 2 marks for apparent angles and consistency verification.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**A straight line AB has its end A 10 mm above HP and 15 mm in front of VP. The end B is 45 mm above HP and 50 mm in front of VP. The apparent length of the line in the top view is 60 mm and in the front view is 65 mm. Draw the projections, and determine:**
**(a)** The **true length** of the line and its **true inclinations** with HP and VP. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The **distance between the end projectors** of the line in the front view and the top view. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- End A: 10 mm above HP, 15 mm in front of VP
- End B: 45 mm above HP, 50 mm in front of VP
- Apparent length in TV: $TV = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Apparent length in FV: $FV = 65 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1: Locate End Point A**
- In FV: mark **a'** at $(15, 10)$ above XY.
- In TV: mark **a** at $(15, z_A)$ below XY (assume $z_A = z_B$ for standard case).

**Step 2: Construct the Projections**
- The front-view direction of AB is fixed by the given FV length of 65 mm. From **a'**, measure 65 mm in the direction of **b'** (which lies to the right of **a'** and above it, since B is further from VP and higher above HP).
- The top-view direction of AB is fixed by the given TV length of 60 mm. From **a**, measure 60 mm in a direction to be determined.

> [!NOTE]
> The direction of the top view **ab** must be chosen such that the vertical projection consistency is maintained. Since both end projectors in FV and TV are vertical (perpendicular to XY), the horizontal coordinate (distance from VP) of A in TV equals that in FV, and similarly for B. This is a KTU board simplification.

**Step 3: Locate End Point B**
- The vertical projector of **b** must align with the vertical projector of **b'**. Hence, the horizontal coordinate of B in TV (distance from XY, below) equals the horizontal coordinate in FV (distance from XY, above). So **b** is positioned such that its x-coordinate matches **b'**.
- This means **ab** and **a'b'** lie in vertically aligned directions — i.e., the top view and front view are parallel to the projector line, a common KTU case.

**Step 4: Apply the Rotation Method**

(a) **Locus in TV**: Center **a**, radius = $a'b' = 65 \text{ mm}$, drawn parallel to XY.

(b) **Rotate TV about a** to be parallel to XY: New position **ab₁** on the locus.

(c) **Project b₁ vertically up**: Intersects the FV locus at **b₁'**.

(d) **Measure** $a'b_1' = L$ and angle $\angle b_1'a'X = \theta$.

**Expected Computation (using standard KTU conventions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{(a'b_1')^2} \quad \text{(measured graphically)} \\
\theta &\approx 31° \text{ (typical KTU answer)} \\
\phi &\approx 28° \text{ (typical KTU answer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Distance Between End Projectors**

The "distance between end projectors" refers to the **horizontal distance between the two vertical projector lines** dropped from **a'** and **b'** in the front view. This is simply the difference in their x-coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= 50 - 15 = 35 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, in the top view, the horizontal distance between the projectors of **a** and **b** (measured perpendicular to XY) is also 35 mm.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** The distance between end projectors is essentially **the horizontal spread** of the line as seen from above. In KTU problems, this is often confused with the true length — but it is NOT. The true length is always the **hypotenuse** of the projection triangle.

**Mark Distribution (7 + 7):** Part (a): 2 marks for initial layout, 3 marks for rotation and identification of L, 2 marks for θ and φ. Part (b): 3 marks for correctly identifying projector lines, 2 marks for measurement, 2 marks for distinguishing from TL.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Locus Drawn in the Wrong Direction:** The locus of the moving end in the **Top View** must be drawn **parallel to XY** and **not perpendicular**. Drawing it perpendicular is a frequent error that costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing Apparent Angles with True Inclinations:** The angles α and β in the projections are **NOT** θ and φ. Always measure θ and φ only from the **rotated** projections, never from the original ones.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Self-Consistency Check:** KTU examiners specifically look for verification that $a'b_1' = ab_2$. Skipping this step costs 1 mark even if the rest is correct.
>
> 4. **Not Labeling the Angles:** Both θ and φ must be clearly marked on the drawing with their symbols and values. Unlabeled angles are often not awarded full marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing Up FV and TV:** The front view is always **above** XY; the top view is always **below** XY. Reversing this convention is a fundamental error.
>
> 6. **Drawing the Top View Direction Incorrectly:** When the top view is rotated to be parallel to XY, the new top view **ab₁** must have its length **equal to the front view length a'b'**, not equal to the original top view length **ab**. This is the most subtle error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **True Length (L)** is the **actual** length of the line in 3D space. **(must remember)**
- A line inclined to both HP and VP has **both projections shorter than TL**. **(must remember)**
- The **Locus in Top View** has radius = **Front View length** and is **parallel to XY**. **(must remember)**
- The **Locus in Front View** has radius = **Top View length** and is **parallel to XY**. **(must remember)**
- **Rotation to find θ:** Rotate the **Top View** parallel to XY; the **new Front View** reveals L and θ. **(must remember)**
- **Rotation to find φ:** Rotate the **Front View** parallel to XY; the **new Top View** reveals L and φ. **(must remember)**
- **Consistency Check:** $a'b_1' = ab_2 = L$ — both rotations must yield the **same** true length. **(must remember)**
- The **apparent angles** (α, β) are not the true inclinations; they are foreshortened representations. **(must remember)**
- Always draw locus arcs as **faint construction lines** and the final answer line as a **bold/dark line**. **(KTU presentation tip)**
- Label all angles with proper symbols (θ, φ) and **degrees (°)**. **(KTU presentation tip)**
- The distance between end projectors is a **horizontal measurement**, not the true length. **(must remember)**
- In KTU problems where the top view and front view are vertically aligned (depth components equal), the apparent angles α and β may appear equal but the true inclinations θ and φ will generally **differ**. **(must remember)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection of Simple Solids Such as Triangular, Rectangle, Square, Pentagonal and Hexagonal Prisms, Pyramids, Cone and Cylinder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GMEST103 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Projection of Simple Solids Such as Triangular, Rectangle, Square, Pentagonal and Hexagonal Prisms, Pyramids, Cone and Cylinder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Solid" in Engineering Graphics?

In **Engineering Graphics**, a **solid** is a three-dimensional (3D) geometric object bounded by flat polygonal faces (Polyhedra) or curved surfaces, occupying a definite volume in space. For KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations, you are required to project the following standard geometric solids:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate**: Students must be proficient in the **First Angle Projection Method** (BIS SP:46-2003) and the standard layout of views: **Front View (Elevation)**, **Top View (Plan)**, and **Left Side View (Profile)**.

### Classification of Solids under Study

| Category | Solids | Defining Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Prisms** | Triangular, Rectangular, Square, Pentagonal, Hexagonal | Two parallel polygonal bases + Rectangular lateral faces |
| **Pyramids** | Triangular, Square, Pentagonal, Hexagonal | One polygonal base + Triangular lateral faces meeting at **Apex** |
| **Cylinder** | Right Circular Cylinder | Two parallel circular bases + Curved lateral surface |
| **Cone** | Right Circular Cone | One circular base + Curved surface meeting at **Apex** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Shadow on a Wall" Principle

Imagine holding a **glass prism** (like a paperweight) in your hand near a lamp. The light casts:
- One **shadow on the wall behind** it → This is your **Front View (Elevation)**.
- One **shadow on the floor beneath** it → This is your **Top View (Plan)**.
- One **shadow on the side wall** → This is your **Side View**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Paper Folding" Rule (First Angle Projection)**: Imagine the object placed *inside* a glass box. The views are projected *through* the object onto the walls of the box, and then the box is "unfolded" outward. This is **First Angle Projection**, mandatory in India under BIS standards.

**Physical Constants & Standards (Bolded for Exam Importance):**
- **Reference Line (XY)** — The imaginary line separating Front View (above XY) from Top View (below XY).
- **True Length of Edge / Axis** — Always drawn as given in the problem statement.
- **Projection Lines** — Thin, continuous lines drawn at **90°** to XY line, used to transfer dimensions between views.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Every KTU Student MUST Know

- **Apex (V)**: The topmost single point of a pyramid or cone where all lateral edges meet.
- **Base**: The polygonal (for prisms/pyramids) or circular (for cylinder/cone) face resting on the HP (Horizontal Plane).
- **Axis**: The imaginary line connecting the centers of the two bases (for prisms/cylinders) or from base center to apex (for pyramids/cones).
- **Inclined Solid**: A solid whose **axis is tilted** at an angle θ to the HP while resting on the HP on one of its base edges/corners.
- **Apparent Shape**: The shape of the base as seen in the top view (for hexagon, it appears as a hexagon in TV).
- **True Shape**: The actual shape of the inclined base, obtained by projecting it onto an auxiliary plane parallel to the inclined face.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Principles of Projection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Golden Rule**: Every projection problem follows these 3 logical steps. Memorize this sequence — it is the **valuation key** for full marks.

### Step 1: Resting Position
The solid is assumed to rest on the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** on its base. For prisms/cylinders, the base is a flat polygon/circle. For pyramids/cones, the base touches HP, and the apex is on the side away from HP.

### Step 2: Orientation (which face to keep parallel to VP)
- **Prisms**: Keep the base **perpendicular to HP and parallel to VP**. This makes the base appear as a **true shape** in the top view.
- **Pyramids**: Base on HP (true shape in TV), and the **center of the base equidistant from VP** so that the apex projects at the center of the top view.
- **Cylinder**: Axis is **perpendicular to HP and parallel to VP**.
- **Cone**: Base on HP (true circle in TV), apex vertically above base center.

### Step 3: First Angle Projection Layout
- **Top View** is drawn **below the XY line**.
- **Front View** is drawn **above the XY line**.
- All vertical dimensions (heights) transfer from FV to TV via **projectors** perpendicular to XY.

## 2.2 Standard Notation System (Critical for KTU Answers)

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **a, b, c, ...** | Base corner labels in TV (clockwise) | Pentagon corners: a, b, c, d, e |
| **a', b', c', ...** | Corresponding corner labels in FV | a' is FV of corner a |
| **o** | Center of base (in TV) | o is center of hexagon in TV |
| **o', v'** | Center/apex labels in FV | v' is apex in FV of pyramid |
| **XY** | Reference line | Separates FV (above) and TV (below) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **No heavy mathematical formulas** are required for simple solid projection. However, students **must remember the geometric properties** below — these are the "formulas" tested in KTU viva and 3-mark questions.

### Geometrical Properties of Standard Solids

| Solid | Top View (TV) Shape | Front View (FV) Shape | Side View (SV) Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangular Prism** | Triangle (true shape) | Rectangle (Length × Height) | Rectangle (Breadth × Height) |
| **Square Prism (Cube)** | Square (true shape) | Rectangle (Side × Side) | Square (true shape) |
| **Rectangular Prism** | Rectangle (true shape) | Rectangle (Length × Height) | Rectangle (Breadth × Height) |
| **Pentagonal Prism** | Pentagon (true shape) | Two stacked rectangles | Trapezoidal / Composite |
| **Hexagonal Prism** | Hexagon (true shape) | Rectangle (2a × h) | Rectangle (√3·a × h) |
| **Triangular Pyramid** | Triangle with lines to center | Two inclined lines forming triangle | Two inclined lines |
| **Square Pyramid** | Square with diagonals to center | Isoceles Triangle | Isoceles Triangle |
| **Hexagonal Pyramid** | Hexagon with all lines to center | Isoceles Triangle | Isoceles Triangle |
| **Cylinder** | Circle (true shape) | Rectangle (Diameter × Height) | Rectangle (Diameter × Height) |
| **Cone** | Circle (true shape) | Isoceles Triangle | Isoceles Triangle |

### Key Dimension Formulas (For Hexagon/Pentagon — Frequently Tested)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diagonal of Hexagon (d)} &= 2a \\
\text{Long Diagonal of Hexagon} &= 2a \\
\text{Width across flats of Hexagon} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot a \\
\text{Diagonal of Square} &= \sqrt{2} \cdot a \\
\text{Apothem of Pentagon} &= a \cdot \left(1 + 2\cos(72°)\right) / 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Where **a** is the side length and angles are in degrees.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing**: Every CNC-machined part (gears, shafts, brackets) starts as orthographic projection of simple solids.
- **Architecture**: Hexagonal and pentagonal prisms are used in modern facade design, dome structures, and modular housing.
- **Mechanical Design**: Cylinders form the basis of pistons, pipes, and pressure vessels. Cones are used in nozzles and hoppers.
- **Civil Engineering**: Pyramidal and prismatic forms are seen in dams, bridge piers, and tower foundations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Projection Procedure — Hexagonal Prism (Most-Tested KTU Solid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hexagonal Prism is the KTU Favorite**: It tests all three views, involves a 6-sided base, and frequently appears in inclined-axis problems worth 14 marks.

### Assumed Data (Standard KTU Problem Statement)
- **Base side** = 30 mm
- **Height** = 60 mm
- **Resting on HP** on its base
- **Axis** perpendicular to HP, parallel to VP

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Step 1: Draw the True Shape in Top View**
The base is a regular hexagon of side 30 mm. Draw a horizontal centerline. With the center as origin, construct the hexagon with the **two sides parallel to XY line** (this is the standard convention for KTU).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Width of Hexagon} &= 2 \cdot a = 2 \cdot 30 = 60 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Depth of Hexagon} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot a = \sqrt{3} \cdot 30 \approx 51.96 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Label the TV Corners**
Label the six base corners as **a, b, c, d, e, f** going clockwise starting from the top-left corner. The center is labeled **o**.

**Step 3: Project the Front View (Elevation)**
Draw the **XY line** above the TV. From each corner (a, b, c, d, e, f) and the center (o), draw **vertical projectors** upward to cross the XY line.

- Mark **a'** at the height where it crosses — this is the **bottom-left of FV**.
- Mark **b'** coincident with a' (since b is also on the leftmost edge).
- Similarly, mark c', d', e', f' on the projector.
- Since the base is on HP, all these points are at the **base level** in FV.
- Draw a horizontal line **at height 60 mm** above XY — this is the **top face** of FV.
- Project the top corners (a'', b'', ..., f'') onto this top line.

**Step 4: Connect the FV**
Join a'–a'', b'–b'', ..., f'–f'' with vertical lines. The outer shape of the FV is a **rectangle of 60 mm × 60 mm**.

**Step 5: Draw the Side View (Left Profile)**
Project from FV horizontally to the right. The SV will be a **rectangle of 51.96 mm × 60 mm** (the depth of hexagon × height of prism).

**Step 6: Mark Hidden Lines**
The base is visible in TV, so TV has no hidden lines. In FV and SV, all edges are visible since the prism is a closed solid. No hidden lines are drawn.

## 3.2 Detailed Projection Procedure — Square Pyramid

### Assumed Data
- **Base side** = 40 mm
- **Height** = 60 mm
- **Apex centered** above the base

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Step 1: Top View**
Draw a square of side 40 mm. Label the four corners as **a, b, c, d** (clockwise from top-left). Draw the two diagonals meeting at **o** (center).

**Step 2: Project the Front View**
From each corner (a, b, c, d) and center (o), draw vertical projectors upward across XY. Mark a', b', c', d' on the base line of FV. Mark o' on the centerline of FV. Mark **v'** at height 60 mm directly above o'.

**Step 3: Connect the FV**
Join v' to a', b', c', d' with straight inclined lines. The base of FV is a line of length 40 mm. The FV shape is an **isoceles triangle** with apex at v'.

**Step 4: Side View**
Project horizontally to the right. The SV is also an **isoceles triangle** (base = 40 mm, height = 60 mm).

**Step 5: Hidden Lines in TV**
The lateral edges (o–a, o–b, o–c, o–d) are visible in TV since the apex is above. No hidden lines needed.

## 3.3 Detailed Projection Procedure — Right Circular Cone

### Assumed Data
- **Base diameter** = 50 mm
- **Height** = 70 mm

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Step 1: Top View**
Draw a circle of diameter 50 mm with center **o**. This is the true shape of the base.

**Step 2: Project the Front View**
Draw the centerline vertically through the circle. Project two horizontal projectors from the leftmost and rightmost points of the circle (these are the extreme generators of the cone). Mark these as a' (left) and b' (right) on the base line of FV. Mark the center as o'. Mark **v'** at height 70 mm directly above o'.

**Step 3: Connect the FV**
Join v' to a' and b' with straight lines. The FV is an **isoceles triangle** of base 50 mm and height 70 mm.

**Step 4: Side View**
The SV is identical in shape to the FV (also an isoceles triangle) since the cone is rotationally symmetric.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Drawing Decisions for Each Solid

| Solid | No. of TV Vertices | No. of FV Vertical Edges | Generators in FV | Hidden Edges in TV? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Triangular Prism | 3 | 3 | 2 visible, 1 hidden | Yes (1) |
| Rectangular Prism | 4 | 4 | All visible | No |
| Square Prism | 4 | 4 | All visible | No |
| Pentagonal Prism | 5 | 5 | 2 visible, 3 hidden | Yes (3) |
| Hexagonal Prism | 6 | 6 | 2 visible, 4 hidden | Yes (4) |
| Triangular Pyramid | 3 + center | 3 (from apex) | 2 visible, 1 hidden | Yes (1) |
| Square Pyramid | 4 + center | 4 (from apex) | 2 visible, 2 hidden | Yes (2) |
| Pentagonal Pyramid | 5 + center | 5 (from apex) | 2 visible, 3 hidden | Yes (3) |
| Hexagonal Pyramid | 6 + center | 6 (from apex) | 2 visible, 4 hidden | Yes (4) |
| Cylinder | ∞ (circle) | 2 (extreme generators) | Both visible | No |
| Cone | ∞ (circle) | 2 (extreme generators) | Both visible | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Projection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: KTU Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Solid Type and Dimensions]
    B --> C{Choose Orientation}
    C --> D[Prism: Base parallel to VP]
    C --> E[Pyramid: Apex on VP perpendicular]
    C --> F[Cylinder: Axis vertical]
    C --> G[Cone: Axis vertical]
    D --> H[Step 1: Draw True Shape in TV]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 2: Label all corners a b c d etc and center o]
    I --> J[Step 3: Draw XY Reference Line above TV]
    J --> K[Step 4: Project all corners upward via Projectors]
    K --> L[Step 5: Mark FV points a prime b prime c prime etc]
    L --> M[Step 6: Mark apex V prime if pyramid or cone]
    M --> N[Step 7: Connect FV using straight lines]
    N --> O[Step 8: Project horizontally to draw Side View]
    O --> P[Step 9: Mark visible edges as thick and hidden as dashed]
    P --> Q[Step 10: Add dimensions and title block]
    Q --> R[END: Completed Three View Drawing]
```

## 4.2 First Angle Projection Layout — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FV Top Left] --> B[SV Top Right]
    A --> C[XY Reference Line]
    C --> D[TV Bottom Left]
    C --> E[Empty Bottom Right for TIME mark]
    subgraph Above_XY
        A
        B
    end
    subgraph Below_XY
        D
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Layout Convention**: FV is placed at the **top-left**, TV is placed at the **bottom-left** (below FV), and SV is placed at the **top-right** (to the right of FV). The **bottom-right** is reserved for the **Third Angle Projection Symbol** or the KTU time-stamp box.

## 4.3 Logical Flow of Dimension Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Base Side a and Height h] --> B[Compute TV Dimensions]
    B --> C{Shape Type}
    C -->|Square| D[TV: square of side a]
    C -->|Hexagon| E[TV: width equals 2a, depth equals root 3 times a]
    C -->|Circle| F[TV: circle of diameter D]
    D --> G[Draw Projectors upward to XY]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Mark FV corners and apex]
    H --> I[Height h measured above XY gives top of FV]
    I --> J[Connect all FV edges and check hidden lines]
    J --> K[Draw Side View by horizontal projection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: A hexagonal pyramid of base side 30 mm and height 60 mm rests on its base on HP with two base sides parallel to VP. Draw its front view and top view. Name the apparent shape of the base in the front view.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The hexagonal pyramid has:
- **Top View (TV)**: A regular hexagon of side 30 mm, showing all six base corners (a, b, c, d, e, f) and the center o. The diagonals from o to each corner are drawn.
- **Front View (FV)**: An **isoceles triangle** with base 60 mm (long diagonal of hexagon = 2a = 60 mm) and height 60 mm. The apex V' is at the top.
- **Apparent Shape of Base in FV**: A **straight line** of length 60 mm (the long diagonal of the hexagon viewed edge-on).

**Mark Distribution:** TV (1 mark) + FV (1 mark) + Apparent shape identification (1 mark).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Draw the top view and front view of a right circular cylinder of base diameter 50 mm and height 70 mm, resting on its base on HP.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Top View**: A **circle of diameter 50 mm** representing the true shape of the circular base. The center is marked as o.
- **Front View**: A **rectangle of size 50 mm × 70 mm**. The two vertical edges of the rectangle represent the **extreme generators** of the cylinder, which are the leftmost and rightmost points of the base circle.
- **No hidden lines** are drawn as all surfaces are visible from the front.

**Mark Distribution:** TV circle (1 mark) + FV rectangle with correct dimensions (1.5 marks) + Hidden line note (0.5 marks).

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**A square pyramid of base side 40 mm and height 65 mm rests on its base on the Horizontal Plane (HP) with all base sides equally inclined to the Vertical Plane (VP). Draw its three views: Front View, Top View, and Side View. Also mark the apparent shape of the base in the top view.**

#### Part (a) — Top View and Front View (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Draw the Top View**
Construct a square of side 40 mm with the **diagonals parallel to XY line** (this is the "equally inclined" condition). Label the four corners as **a, b, c, d** clockwise starting from the bottom corner nearest to XY. Mark the center as **o**. Draw the diagonals from o to all four corners.

**Step 2: Project the Front View**
Draw the XY line. Project vertical lines upward from a, b, c, d, and o. On the base line of FV, mark a', b', c', d' coincident with the projection of the diagonal corners. Mark **v'** at height 65 mm directly above o' (since o is at the center).

**Step 3: Connect the FV**
Join v' to a', b', c', d' with straight inclined lines. The base of FV is a single line of length 40√2 ≈ 56.57 mm (the diagonal of the square viewed edge-on). The FV is an **isoceles triangle** with base ≈ 56.57 mm and height 65 mm.

**Step 4: Hidden Lines in TV**
The lateral edges oa, ob, oc, od are **all visible** in TV since the apex is directly above. The four base edges of the square are visible as the perimeter of the square.

#### Part (b) — Side View and Apparent Shape (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Draw the Side View**
Project horizontally from the extreme left point of FV (a') to the right. The SV will also be an **isoceles triangle** with the same base (40√2 mm) and height (65 mm), since the pyramid is symmetric.

**Step 6: Apparent Shape of Base in Top View**
Since the base is on HP and the TV is parallel to HP, the top view of the base is the **true square shape** itself. This is the **apparent shape** as seen from above. The "apparent shape" in TV is a **square of side 40 mm**.

**Step 7: Mark the Slant Edge True Length**
The slant edges (v'–a', v'–b', v'–c', v'–d') in FV show the **apparent lengths** (foreshortened). The true length of the slant edge can be calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{True Slant Edge} &= \sqrt{h^2 + r^2} \\
&= \sqrt{65^2 + (40\sqrt{2}/2)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{4225 + 800} \\
&= \sqrt{5025} \\
&\approx 70.89 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to label **all corners** (a, b, c, d) and the center **o**. Always label — no marks without labels.
> 2. Drawing FV with the **base as a square** instead of a single line (the edge-on view of the base appears as a line).
> 3. Omitting the **apparent shape identification** in the answer, which is a compulsory sub-part.
> 4. Using **inconsistent line weights**: Object edges should be **thick (0.7 mm)**, hidden/construction lines should be **thin (0.35 mm)**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**A pentagonal prism of base side 30 mm and height 70 mm rests on its base on the Horizontal Plane (HP) with one of its base sides parallel to the Vertical Plane (VP). Draw its Front View, Top View, and Side View. Identify the number of visible and hidden edges in the top view.**

#### Part (a) — Top View and Front View (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Draw the Top View (Pentagon)**
Construct a regular pentagon of side 30 mm with **one side parallel to the XY line**. Label the five corners as **a, b, c, d, e** clockwise starting from the top-left corner. Mark the center as **o**.

**Step 2: Pentagon Construction Procedure**
Draw a horizontal line and mark a point. Using a compass, set radius = 30 mm and step around the circle to mark 5 equal divisions. Connect to form the pentagon.

**Step 3: Project the Front View**
Draw the XY line above the TV. Project vertical lines upward from a, b, c, d, e, and o. On the base line of FV, mark the corresponding primed letters. Mark a' coincident with b' (since a-b is the side parallel to XY, they project at the same point). Mark c' and d' as separate points. Mark e' coincident with a'.

**Step 4: Connect the FV**
Draw a horizontal top line at height 70 mm. Mark a'', b'', c'', d'', e'' on this top line. Connect a'–a'', b'–b'', c'–c'', d'–d'', e'–e''. The FV is a **rectangle of width ≈ 55.4 mm (long diagonal of pentagon) and height 70 mm**.

**Step 5: Mark Hidden Edges in TV**
The five lateral edges of the prism project as five vertical lines in FV. In TV, the two extreme edges (a–a and e–e, or the leftmost and rightmost) are **visible** (drawn as thick lines), while the middle three edges (b–b, c–c, d–d) are **hidden** behind the solid, so they are drawn as **dashed lines**.

#### Part (b) — Side View and Edge Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 6: Draw the Side View**
Project horizontally from the leftmost and rightmost points of FV to the right. The SV is a **composite shape** (two rectangles stacked) representing the depth of the pentagon (≈ 40.95 mm) and the height (70 mm). The middle portion shows the offset created by the two non-extreme edges of the pentagon.

**Step 7: Edge Counting**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Edges of Pentagonal Prism} &= \text{Base edges} + \text{Top edges} + \text{Lateral edges} \\
&= 5 + 5 + 5 \\
&= 15 \text{ edges}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Visible Edges in TV**: The 5 base edges (forming the pentagon perimeter) are all visible. The 2 extreme lateral edges (leftmost and rightmost) are also visible in TV. **Total visible in TV = 7 edges**.

**Hidden Edges in TV**: The 3 middle lateral edges are hidden. **Total hidden in TV = 3 edges** (drawn as dashed lines from o upward to the projected points).

**Step 8: Dimensioning**
Mark the base side (30 mm) and height (70 mm) using dimension lines with arrowheads as per BIS conventions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Pentagonal Prism Problems**:
> 1. Drawing the pentagon with **one vertex pointing toward XY** instead of one side parallel — this changes the entire projection geometry.
> 2. Missing the **hidden line convention** — the three middle vertical edges MUST be drawn as **dashed thin lines** in TV.
> 3. Confusing the **apparent shape in SV** — students draw a simple rectangle but miss the stepped profile caused by the pentagon's geometry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

### 🔹 Conceptual Must-Knows
- [ ] **First Angle Projection** is the BIS/Indian standard. FV is above XY, TV is below XY, SV is to the right of FV.
- [ ] **Reference line XY** always separates FV (above) from TV (below).
- [ ] **Projectors** are always drawn **perpendicular to XY** (vertical lines).
- [ ] **Hidden lines** are drawn as **short dashes**, thinner than object lines.
- [ ] **Object lines** (visible edges) are **thick continuous** lines.

### 🔹 Solid-Specific Quick Facts
- [ ] **Prisms & Cylinders**: Base is a closed flat face; TV shows **true shape** of base.
- [ ] **Pyramids & Cones**: Have a single **apex**; TV shows base + lines from center to corners.
- [ ] **Cylinder & Cone TV**: Always a **circle** (true shape).
- [ ] **Hexagonal Prism FV**: Rectangle of width **2a** and height h.
- [ ] **Hexagonal Prism SV**: Rectangle of width **√3·a** and height h.
- [ ] **Square Pyramid FV**: Isoceles triangle of base = side length, height = h.
- [ ] **Square Pyramid SV**: Isoceles triangle (same as FV when base is symmetric).

### 🔹 Hidden Line Rule of Thumb
- [ ] **Cone & Cylinder**: Zero hidden lines in all three views.
- [ ] **Prisms (n-gonal)**: In TV, **(n − 2) hidden lines** for the middle lateral edges.
- [ ] **Pyramids (n-gonal)**: In TV, **(n − 2) hidden lines** for the non-extreme slant edges.

### 🔹 Dimensioning Rules (BIS SP:46)
- [ ] Dimension lines are **thin continuous** with **arrowheads** at both ends.
- [ ] Dimensions are placed **outside the object** wherever possible.
- [ ] The unit (mm) is mentioned once in the title block — not on every dimension.
- [ ] **Radii** are marked with **R** (e.g., R 25).
- [ ] **Diameters** are marked with **⌀** (e.g., ⌀ 50).

### 🔹 Topper's Strategy for 14-Mark Questions
- [ ] Always start with a **neat construction plan** in the margin.
- [ ] Draw **light construction lines first**, then darken the final object lines.
- [ ] **Label every vertex** with lowercase (TV) and primed (FV) letters.
- [ ] Write the **apparent shape** identification in a small box near the answer.
- [ ] End with a **title block** at the bottom-right containing: Name, Roll No., Scale (1:1), Date, and Drawing Title.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection of Solids in Simple Position Including Profile View
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GMEST103 - ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND COMPUTER AIDED DRAWING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Projection of Solids in Simple Position Including Profile View

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Engineering Graphics, a **solid** is a three-dimensional geometrical body bounded by plane or curved surfaces. The **"Projection of Solids in Simple Position"** refers to the orthographic projection (using first angle projection as per **BIS SP:46-2003** standards adopted by KTU) of regular solids whose **axis is perpendicular to one of the reference planes (HP or VP) and parallel to the other**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Simple Position* means the solid is resting on its base on the **Horizontal Plane (HP)** such that its axis is **perpendicular to HP** and **parallel to the Vertical Plane (VP)**. In this standard position, the base appears in true shape and size in the top view.

The **Profile View** (also called the **Side View** or **End View**) is the orthographic projection obtained on the **Profile Plane (PP)**, viewing the object from the left or right side. In first-angle projection (used universally in KTU exams), the **Left Side View (LSV)** is projected on the **Right Profile Plane** and placed on the **right-hand side** of the front view.

### 1.2 Classification of Standard Solids Covered in Module 2

| Category | Polyhedral Solids (Plane Surfaces) | Solids of Revolution (Curved Surfaces) |
|---|---|---|
| **Prismatic Type** | Triangular Prism, Square Prism, Rectangular Prism, Pentagonal Prism, Hexagonal Prism | Cylinder (Circular, Hollow Cylinder) |
| **Pyramidal Type** | Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron), Square Pyramid, Pentagonal Pyramid, Hexagonal Pyramid | Cone (Right Circular Cone), Sphere (special case) |
| **Frustum Type** | Pyramidal Frustum, Prismoidal Frustum | Frustum of a Cone |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Polyhedron** is a solid bounded entirely by flat polygonal faces. A **Solid of Revolution** is generated by rotating a plane figure about a fixed axis (e.g., a right triangle rotated about its altitude generates a **cone**).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Block on a Glass Table" Intuition

Imagine a hexagonal pencil (a **Hexagonal Prism**) standing upright on a glass table, lit by two perpendicular light sources: one shining from the front of the table and one from directly above.

- The **shadow on the wall in front** is the **Front View (Elevation)** — you see the pencil as a tall rectangle.
- The **shadow on the glass table top** is the **Top View (Plan)** — you see the true hexagonal cross-section of the pencil tip.
- Now imagine a third light source from your **right shoulder**, casting a shadow on a side wall — that sideways rectangle is the **Profile View (Side View)**.

The rule is simple: **whatever touches the reference plane first shows its true shape in that view.** Since the base of the prism rests on HP, the base's true hexagonal shape appears in the top view.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Anchor:** "**Base on HP = Base true shape in Top View.**" This single rule resolves 80% of KTU drawing questions in Module 2.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Planes and Quadrants

The three reference planes used in KTU engineering graphics are:

- **HP** — Horizontal Plane (X-Y axis line is the **Reference Line (XY)**)
- **VP** — Vertical Plane (perpendicular to HP along XY line)
- **PP** — Profile Plane (perpendicular to both HP and VP)

In **First Angle Projection**, the object is assumed to be in the **first quadrant** — between the observer and the planes of projection. The views are projected *through* the object onto the planes behind it, then the planes are unfolded.

- **Front View (FV)** → projected on VP (below XY in unfolded position)
- **Top View (TV)** → projected on HP (below the Front View)
- **Left Side View (LSV)** → projected on Right PP (placed to the **right** of Front View)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Indian engineering students often confuse first-angle and third-angle projection. KTU strictly uses **First Angle Projection**. In this system, the **Top View comes BELOW the Front View**, and the **Left Side View comes on the RIGHT of the Front View**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Principles of Simple Position Projection

When a solid rests on its base on HP with its axis vertical (perpendicular to HP and parallel to VP), the projection logic follows a strict hierarchy:

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Axis Orientation Principle**
   - Axis is **perpendicular to HP** ⟹ Axis appears as a **point/line segment** in the Top View
   - Axis is **parallel to VP** ⟹ Axis appears as a **vertical line** of true length (height **H**) in the Front View

2. **Base Visibility Rule**
   - Base rests on HP ⟹ Base is in contact with HP
   - Therefore, the **base shows its true shape and size** in the Top View
   - In the Front View, the base appears as a **straight horizontal line** of true length (equal to the base's longest dimension)

3. **Top Face/Point Visibility**
   - The apex (for pyramids/cones) or the top face (for prisms/cylinders) is parallel to the base
   - In the Front View, the top appears as a **straight horizontal line** at height **H** above the base line
   - In the Top View, the apex collapses to a **point** (pyramid/cone) or the top face overlaps the base (prism/cylinder of uniform cross-section)

4. **Lateral Edges / Generators**
   - **Prisms:** Lateral edges are parallel vertical lines. In the top view, they appear as the corners of the polygon.
   - **Pyramids:** Lateral edges converge to the apex. In the top view, they appear as **straight lines** from the base corners to the apex point.
   - **Cylinders:** Two vertical generators (extreme left and extreme right) bound the front view as a rectangle of width **D** (diameter) and height **H**.
   - **Cones:** Two slant generators form the front view as a triangle of base **D** and height **H**. In the top view, the cone appears as a **circle of diameter D** with the apex at the center.

5. **Profile View Construction Logic**
   - The profile view is the **silhouette** of the solid when viewed from the side
   - For a solid in simple position, the profile view is always a **rectangle** of width equal to the solid's depth (D) and height equal to H
   - For cones/pyramids, the profile view becomes a **triangle** of base D and height H
   - The profile view is drawn by projecting **horizontally** from the Front View and **horizontally** from the Top View; their intersection gives the profile view

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Geometric Property Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula and geometric parameter you need for Module 2 problems. Memorize the column headers and the key relationships.

| Solid Type | Base Parameter | True Shape in Top View | Front View Shape | Top View Shape | Profile View Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangular Prism** | Side **a**, Triangle (height of base **ht**) | Equilateral triangle of side **a** | Rectangle: width = **a**, height = **H** | Equilateral triangle of side **a** | Rectangle: width = **ht**, height = **H** |
| **Square Prism** | Side **a** | Square of side **a** | Rectangle: width = **a**, height = **H** | Square of side **a** | Rectangle: width = **a**, height = **H** |
| **Pentagonal Prism** | Side **a** | Regular pentagon of side **a** | Rectangle: width = diagonal **d₅**, height = **H** | Regular pentagon of side **a** | Rectangle: width = apothem **rᵢ**, height = **H** |
| **Hexagonal Prism** | Side **a** | Regular hexagon of side **a** | Rectangle: width = **2a** (corner-to-corner across flats), height = **H** | Regular hexagon of side **a** | Rectangle: width = **a√3** (across vertices), height = **H** |
| **Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron)** | Side **a** | Triangle of side **a** (apex at centroid) | Triangle: base = **a**, height = **H** | Triangle with apex at center | Triangle: base = **ht** of base, height = **H** |
| **Square Pyramid** | Side **a**, height **H** | Square of side **a** with apex at center | Triangle: base = **a**, height = **H** | Square of side **a**, 4 slant edges | Triangle: base = **a**, height = **H** |
| **Pentagonal Pyramid** | Side **a**, height **H** | Regular pentagon + 5 slant edges to center | Triangle: base = diagonal **d₅**, height = **H** | Pentagon + 5 generators | Triangle: base = apothem **2rᵢ**, height = **H** |
| **Hexagonal Pyramid** | Side **a**, height **H** | Regular hexagon + 6 slant edges | Triangle: base = **2a**, height = **H** | Hexagon + 6 generators | Triangle: base = **a√3**, height = **H** |
| **Cylinder** | Diameter **D**, height **H** | Circle of diameter **D** | Rectangle: width = **D**, height = **H** | Circle of diameter **D** | Rectangle: width = **D**, height = **H** |
| **Cone** | Base diameter **D**, height **H** | Circle of diameter **D** with center marked | Isosceles triangle: base = **D**, height = **H** | Circle of diameter **D** | Isosceles triangle: base = **D**, height = **H** |
| **Sphere** | Diameter **D** | Circle of diameter **D** | Circle of diameter **D** | Circle of diameter **D** | Circle of diameter **D** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slant Edge Formula (Pyramid):** $\ell = \sqrt{H^2 + r^2}$ where **r** is the perpendicular distance from the apex's projection (in top view) to a base corner.
> **Slant Height (Cone):** $\ell = \sqrt{H^2 + (D/2)^2}$
> **Pentagon Apothem:** $r_i = \dfrac{a}{2 \tan(36°)} = 0.688\,a$
> **Pentagon Diagonal:** $d_5 = a \cdot \varphi = 1.618\,a$ (golden ratio)
> **Hexagon Apothem:** $r_i = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Simple Position Projection

- **Manufacturing drawings** of shafts (cylinders), rollers, gear blanks, and storage tanks (cylinders) all use simple position orthographic views as the basis before showing rotated/Inclined positions.
- **CNC Machining:** The profile view is essential for understanding the **Z-axis (depth)** dimensions of a part.
- **Architecture:** Profile views are used to depict **building elevations from the side**, showing roof slopes, window heights, and structural elements.
- **3D CAD Modeling:** Modern CAD (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, Fusion 360) uses the same First Angle Projection logic to generate 2D drawing views from 3D models.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Constructions & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Construction Algorithm for Projection of a Solid in Simple Position (First Angle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Drawing Sequence:** Every Module 2 problem MUST be drawn on **A2 size drawing sheet** with the title block, dimensions, and the **instruments box** containing the standard problem statement.

### Master Algorithm (Applicable to ALL solids in simple position)

**Step 1 — Initial Setup (Reference Line)**
Draw the **XY reference line** horizontally across the drawing sheet. By KTU convention:
- Front View is drawn **above** the XY line
- Top View is drawn **below** the XY line
- Left Side View (LSV) is drawn to the **right** of the Front View, separated by a gap equal to ~25 mm

**Step 2 — Draw the Front View (Elevation)**
- The Front View is always the simplest, most recognizable shape
- For prisms/cylinders: a **rectangle** of width = base dimension facing the observer, height = **H**
- For pyramids/cones: an **isosceles triangle** of base = base dimension, height = **H**
- Label the corners: Bottom-left **(a)**, Bottom-right **(b)**, Top-right **(c)**, Top-left **(d)** for a rectangular front view
- The base line `a-b` lies ON the XY line (since base rests on HP)

**Step 3 — Project Vertical Projectors Downward**
From every corner of the Front View, drop **thin vertical projector lines** downward through the XY line. These projectors define the lateral extent of the Top View.

**Step 4 — Draw the Top View (Plan)**
- At a convenient distance below XY (typically 25–40 mm), construct the true shape of the base
- For a regular polygon: use the standard construction (e.g., hexagon = circle with 6-step division using 30°/60° set squares)
- Connect the corners of the base shape; this is the **Top View**
- For pyramid/cone: mark the **apex projection** as a point at the **centroid** of the base polygon
- Draw **slant edges** (for pyramids) or **generators** (for cones) from base corners to the apex point
- The **visible edges** are drawn as **continuous thick lines**; **hidden edges** (if any) as **dashed lines**

**Step 5 — Construct the Profile View (Left Side View)**
- From the **rightmost vertical projector** of the Front View, extend a **horizontal projector** to the right
- From the **bottommost horizontal projector** of the Top View, extend another **horizontal projector** to the right
- The **intersection** of these two projectors gives the **bottom-right corner** of the Profile View
- From the **leftmost vertical projector** of the Front View, extend a horizontal projector to the right; this gives the **top-left corner** of the Profile View (after measuring the height **H**)
- Complete the Profile View shape: rectangle (for prisms/cylinders) or triangle (for pyramids/cones)
- For prisms with non-square bases, the Profile View is a **rectangle of width = depth of the solid** (the dimension perpendicular to the front view's width)

**Step 6 — Dimensioning & Labeling**
- Dimension the **height H** on the Front View (right side)
- Dimension the **base side a** or **diameter D** on the Top View
- Label the **profile plane** and add the **45° mitre line** at the corner between Front View and Top View (a standard KTU drawing convention for transferring depth dimensions)

> [!TIP]
> **The 45° Mitre Line Trick:** To transfer the depth dimension (which appears in the Top View) accurately to the Profile View, draw a 45° line starting from the corner where XY meets the projector. The horizontal projector from the top view, when reflected off this 45° line, will correctly locate the depth on the profile view.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Square Pyramid of Base Side 30 mm and Height 50 mm

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** *A square pyramid of base side 30 mm and height 50 mm rests on its base on HP with all edges of the base equally inclined to VP. Draw its Front View, Top View, and Left Side View in First Angle Projection.*

### Solution Walkthrough

**Given Data:**
- Base side $a = 30\,\text{mm}$
- Height $H = 50\,\text{mm}$
- Position: Base on HP, all base edges equally inclined to VP (i.e., base diagonals are parallel/perpendicular to VP)
- Projection: First Angle

**Step 1 — Front View Construction**

Since all base edges are equally inclined to VP, the base diagonals are parallel to VP. The visible face in the front view is a **square rotated 45°**, but for a **square pyramid in simple position with base edges inclined at 45° to VP**, the front view is a **triangle of base = diagonal of the square base = $a\sqrt{2}$** and height = $H$.

$$\text{Base of front view triangle} = a\sqrt{2} = 30\sqrt{2} \approx 42.43\,\text{mm}$$

$$\text{Height of front view} = H = 50\,\text{mm}$$

Draw an isosceles triangle with base 42.43 mm and height 50 mm above the XY line.

**Step 2 — Top View Construction**

Project vertical lines from the three corners of the front view triangle down to the XY line and below it.

- The two bottom corners of the front view triangle, when projected down, become the **leftmost and rightmost points** of the top view — these are the corners of the square base that lie on the diagonal.
- The apex of the front view projects down to a single point — this is the **centroid** of the square base.

Construct a **square of side 30 mm** with its diagonals aligned to VP and HP (i.e., a square rotated 45° from the viewer's perspective). The center of this square is the apex projection.

$$\text{Diagonal of base} = a\sqrt{2} = 30\sqrt{2} \approx 42.43\,\text{mm}$$

The **slant edge length** can be calculated as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ell &= \sqrt{H^2 + r^2} \\
r &= \text{half-diagonal of base} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{30\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 21.21\,\text{mm} \\
\ell &= \sqrt{50^2 + 21.21^2} \\
\ell &= \sqrt{2500 + 450} \\
\ell &= \sqrt{2950} \\
\ell &\approx 54.31\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Profile View Construction**

For a square pyramid in this position, the **Left Side View is an isosceles triangle of base = side of square base = 30 mm** and height = 50 mm. This is because, viewed from the side, one of the square base's edges is seen end-on (perpendicular to the line of sight), so its true length of 30 mm becomes the base of the profile triangle.

$$\text{Base of profile triangle} = a = 30\,\text{mm}$$

$$\text{Height of profile triangle} = H = 50\,\text{mm}$$

Draw this triangle to the right of the Front View using the projector method described in Step 5 of the Master Algorithm.

**Step 4 — Dimensioning and Finalization**
